News.title: Brits swap lads' holidays for 'mum trips' as new travel trend emerges
News.content:
Brits swap lads' holidays for 'mum trips' as new travel trend emerges

One upon a time blokes couldn't wait to cut the apron strings and jet off with their mates, but now mums are right up there as favourites among holiday companions

View 3 Images Mum's the word when it comes to holiday's nowadays (Image: Getty Images)

Travel trends have been dominated by lads’ holiday and girls’ trips for years.

‌



But Brits are increasingly swapping group getaways for time away with the person who took them on their first ever holiday: their mum. New research from Heathrow Express reveals that 30% of adults have been away with their mum, parent or carer in the last three years.

‌



Meanwhile 16% are planning a holiday with their mum in the next year, fuelling the rise of bonding trips based on connection. The research shows a growing desire for spending quality time with the people you love - nearly 60% worry they’re not making enough time for their mum, parent or carer as they get older.

‌



View 3 Images Brits are much more likely to enjoy a break with parents now (Image: Getty Images)

Five last minute Mother's Day gift experiences mum will love more than flowers READ MORE :

Locals puzzled as strange structures pop up on popular UK beach READ MORE :

Those who do travel with parents believe there are real benefits, with 38% saying it’s a more relaxing experience than going away with friends. There are also long term advantages including creating lasting memories (37%) strengthening relationships (29%) and helping with reconnection (28%).

‌



Aoife Considine, business lead at Heathrow Express, said: “Travel has a wonderful way of pressing pause on everyday life. When you’re away together, there’s space for conversations you don’t normally have and moments you don’t always make time for at home.

View 3 Images Holidaying with our parents is seen as a chance to reconnect by many (Image: Getty Images)

“For many adults, a trip with their mum or parent figure isn’t just another holiday – it’s a chance to reconnect, laugh about old stories and create new ones. Those are the kinds of memories people carry with them long after they’ve returned home.”

Article continues below

As families celebrate Mother’s Day, Heathrow Express is giving travellers 20% off from March 14 to 21 by using the discount code HEXMARCH.

Those jetting off over the Mother’s Day weekend itself can also get a complimentary dessert with any main course bought at Gordon Ramsay’s Plane Food Market at Heathrow Terminal 5.

News.title: Colon cancer symptoms as MAFS star shares heartbreaking update
News.content:
Colon cancer symptoms as MAFS star shares heartbreaking update

Mel Schilling said her light is ‘starting to fade’ as the cancer has metastasized

View 3 Images Mel Schilling shared a devastating health update on social media (Image: GETTY)

Mel Schilling revealed that after three years and countless rounds of chemotherapy, her cancer has spread from her colon. Colon and rectum cancer affects around 44,000 people in the UK each year and is becoming increasingly common in people over the age of 50.

‌



The 54-year-old TV personality revealed to fans over social media that now her cancer has metastasised, “my oncology team have now told me there is nothing further they can do”. Mel’s cancer journey started in December 2023 when she was diagnosed with colon cancer after finding a tumour “the size of a lemon”.

‌



The tumour, which she nicknamed Terry, was removed and she was given the all-clear before a routine scan a few months later discovered the cancer had spread to her lungs. She continued chemotherapy while filming Married At First Sight over the last two years and was told she’d be eligible for a groundbreaking trial due to start in March 2026.

‌



However, after experiencing headaches and numbness over Christmas, new scans revealed the cancer had metastasised to her brain too. The news comes after Mel stepped back from the Australian reality programme after spending 12 seasons on its expert panel.

Metastasised means a cancer has spread from the original site it was found in to other locations. This happens when cancer cells detach from the primary site and travel, usually through the bloodstream or lymphatic systems, to other parts of the body and form new tumours there.

View 3 Images Mel was first diagnosed and treated for colon cancer in 2023 (Image: GETTY)

‌



Cancer can metastasise at any stage, not just when the disease has advanced. Some may even spread before the original tumour is detected.

Mel added in her heartbreaking announcement: “If I could leave you with one thing, it would simply be this: if something doesn’t feel right, please get it checked out. It might just save your life.”

Cancer experts at Macmillan urge people to see their GPs if they experience blood in their stools, bleeding from the back passage or a change in their normal bowel habit, as these are common signs of colon cancer.

‌



Common signs of bowel cancer:

Changes in your stool, such as diarrhoea or constipation that is not usual for you

Needing the bathroom more or less often than usual

Often feeling like you need to the bathroom, even if you've just been

Tummy pain

A lump in your tummy

Bloating

Losing weight without trying

Feeling very tired or short of breath

View 3 Images Bowel cancer is one of the most common types of cancer in the UK (Image: GETTY)

‌



44,100 people are affected by colon or rectum cancer each year, according to the charity. While this type of cancer is more common in older people, the number of people under the age of 50 being diagnosed is increasing.

The exact causes of colon cancer are not fully understood, but there are risk factors that may increase your chances of developing it, particularly if you have more than one.

Risk factors include:

Article continues below

News.title: 'You can’t out-parent a social media app built on addiction' say campaigners
News.content:
'You can’t out-parent a social media app built on addiction' say campaigners

Teens are growing up online, but the risks are rising faster than the rules. With under-16 bans under discussion, parents, politicians, and experts are asking: what actually keeps young people safe?

View 2 Images A proposed ban on social media for under-16s has been rejected by MPs.

Fourteen-year-old Evie Thomas tried to log onto Snapchat, only to be greeted with the dreaded “Your account has been locked” message. In Australia, where a nationwide ban on under-16s accessing major social media platforms came into effect last December, this has become a familiar experience for many teens. Yet Evie's habits haven’t changed.

‌



In an interview with ITV News she explains: “Saying no, you can’t do something and forcefully taking that away without giving them much choice really makes people want to do it more”. Her words underscore a tension at the heart of the growing debate over whether banning social media for children truly protects them or simply drives them underground.

‌



Australia’s experiment has drawn international attention, with 15-year-old Noah Jones taking the government to court, arguing that while accounts were deactivated, harmful content and its creators remain online. The country’s early results are mixed: authorities report nearly five million accounts deactivated, but critics warn the measure may have unintended consequences, from teens migrating to less-regulated platforms to privacy concerns around strict age verification.

‌



View 2 Images MPs back government bid to give extra powers to the Secretary of State, keeping the door open for a future social media ban for under-16s.

In the UK, policymakers are watching closely. MPs recently rejected a call for an Australia-style ban, instead backing flexible ministerial powers that could allow the Labour government to restrict children’s access to social media features or platforms deemed harmful. The vote - 307 to 173 against a full ban - highlights the difficulty ministers face in regulating children’s online lives.

Labour minister Olivia Bailey said the government’s consultation would “seek views to help shape our next steps”, which could still include restricting children’s access to platforms such as Instagram and TikTok.

‌



Meanwhile, Conservative Shadow Education Secretary Laura Trott described the situation as “an emergency”. Separate polling has suggested around 40% of children are exposed to explicit content during the school day, adding to pressure on ministers to act.

‘Parents vs social media’s addictive design'

Experts warn that the challenges of social media extend far beyond screen time. Research from National Library of Medicine shows that spending more than three hours a day on social media can double the risk of poor mental health outcomes for 12- to 15-year-olds, contributing to anxiety, depression, and sleep disruption. Self-esteem can be affected, particularly when teens compare themselves to the curated “highlight reels” of others, and exposure to cyberbullying, violent or sexual content, and digital over-reliance further compounds the risks.

For parents, the stakes are high. Justine Roberts, founder of Mumsnet and a campaigner for an under-16 ban, says families are struggling to manage the addictive nature of social media platforms. “Parents talk about cancelling phone contracts or removing devices altogether, only to see the problem reappear through peer pressure or workarounds. You can’t out-parent a business model built on addiction,” she says.

‌



Surveys of Mumsnet users suggest 61% of parents believe their child is addicted to their phone or social media, while 83% support government intervention to restrict access. Roberts adds that legal restrictions could help parents by making it socially acceptable to limit their children’s use. Research from Pew Research Center shows teenagers mostly use Instagram, Snapchat, and TikTok to stay connected with friends, reinforcing the role of mainstream platforms in social life.

Social media platforms are designed to maximise user engagement. Every scroll, pause, and click feeds data into algorithms that learn what keeps users on the platform for longer. Scott Baxter, co-owner and technical director of First Internet, says this design can shape what people see online. For younger users whose brains are still developing, he argues, algorithm-driven feeds can amplify fears, insecurities and curiosity - though the effects are not limited to children.

Baxter says that while an outright ban may not be perfect, stronger age restrictions combined with platform accountability and parental controls could reduce children’s exposure to high-risk environments

‌



‘ Would a ban actually work?'

The evidence on bans remains mixed. Some researchers argue that limiting access could reduce children’s exposure to harmful content, but others warn about unintended consequences. Marcantonio Spada, professor of addictive behaviours and mental health at London South Bank University, has warned that strict bans could push teenagers towards less regulated platforms, reduce opportunities to develop digital literacy, and raise privacy concerns if age verification systems require extensive personal data.

Spada has suggested that a lower age threshold - closer to 13, which aligns with the minimum age used by many platforms - may strike a more practical balance between reducing risk and avoiding unintended side effects. Other mental-health practitioners working with adolescents emphasise that education and digital literacy remain essential, arguing that young people also need guidance on how to navigate online spaces safely rather than being completely shielded from them.

Some technology specialists also argue that partial regulation may still have value. Scott Baxter, co-owner and technical director of First Internet, says reducing children’s exposure to the largest and most algorithm-driven platforms could still make a meaningful difference, even if a ban cannot eliminate online risks entirely.

‌



‘ Making growing up online safer'

The UK government has launched a public consultation on whether to restrict social media use for under-16s, open from 2 March to 26 May 2026. Parents, young people, educators, children’s charities, academics, and technology providers are invited to share their views on a range of measures, from a full ban on platforms like Instagram and TikTok to less dramatic interventions such as switching off addictive features like autoplay, introducing overnight curfews, limiting AI chatbot access, or strengthening age verification. Technology Secretary Liz Kendall said the consultation aims to understand how young people can “thrive in an age of rapid technological change.”

Evie and Noah’s experiences, combined with research and parental concerns, show that growing up online is now an integral part of adolescence. The challenge is not simply to switch off screens, but to create safer environments, provide practical guidance, and help young people develop resilience both on- and offline.

“Growing up online is part of childhood itself, and the challenge isn’t to switch off screens completely, but to ensure the environment is safe, the guidance is practical, and young people can thrive both on and offline,” says campaigner Justine Roberts.

Article continues below

Whether through education, carefully designed regulation, or platform accountability, the debate over social media and under-16s tests society’s ability to navigate childhood in a digital age.

News.title: Mission accomplished? The 2003 boast that haunts today's Iran conflict
News.content: Iraq is now in a much better state than it was in the immediate aftermath and many are glad to see Saddam Hussein gone. But democracy did not spread through the Middle East in the aftermath as some had claimed it would. Instead, one of the biggest winners of the invasion would be Iran whose main adversary was removed, allowing it to extend its influence into Iraq and beyond in the years after the war. And it would increase the terrorist threat within the UK and the broader West. Wars do not always have the outcomes people expect or want.
